metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the per-criterion invocation entry-point that `rebar criteria eval <id>` calls — either a new thin wrapper over the plan-review Pass-1 finder for a single criterion (e.g. `run_criterion(cid, plan_text, *, runner, repo_root)`) or an explicit spike to validate that the existing `case_store` + orchestrator seam supports single-criterion fire/no-fire evaluation before committing the eval-runner AC shape.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `rebar criteria eval <id>` can be delivered by 'extending the closed solver dispatch' in `eval_solver.run_case`, but the existing solver's invocation model is incompatible with plan-review criteria: `run_case` dispatches to three standalone reviewer ops (`completion.verify_completion`, `operations.review_ticket`, `spec_scan.scan_epics_for_spec`) each with a `repo_root + runner` seam, while plan-review criteria execute as rubric chunks through `orchestrator.run_review` / the four-pass workflow. There is no per-criterion callable today that accepts a `{criterion_id, fixture}` and returns a `fire | no-fire` verdict. The 'extend the closed dispatch' path needs a net-new per-criterion invocation entry-point (not just a new case in the existing dispatch), and the plan does not specify or spike it.
findings.location: § What exists vs. what we build — 'Per-criterion eval runner — `eval_solver.run_case` is a closed 3-id dispatch (raises otherwise) + a validate-only CLI; add a criterion-aware runner + a live CLI'; AC: `rebar criteria eval <id> runs a project criterion's must-fire/must-not-fire…`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a single canonical `sanitize_criterion_id(cid: str) -> str` function (e.g. in `registry.py`) that both `plan_review_assemble_criteria` (Python key builder) and the YAML gate generator use, and specify where/how YAML batch criterion entries for project ids are generated (static regeneration vs runtime dynamic batch membership).
findings.finding: The sanitization rule for `project.<name>` criterion ids → `include_project_<name>` workflow output keys is stated once in prose but not co-located with the two sites that must apply it consistently: (a) the Python `plan_review_assemble_criteria` op that builds `f'include_{cid}'` keys, and (b) the YAML gate definition that has static `when: '${{ steps.assemble.outputs.include_<ID> }}'` batch entries. The plan says project ids enter the vocabulary at 'workflow-definition time' but does not specify which module owns the canonical sanitized name or how the static YAML batch criterion list gets extended.
findings.location: § Overlay schema — 'the include_<id> output key must sanitize the project.<name> id, e.g. .→_, to a valid workflow output key'; AC: 'effective_criteria(repo_root)…project ids must enter it too'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `sanitize_criterion_id` to `registry.py` (e.g. `cid.replace('.', '_').replace('-', '_')`); have `plan_review_assemble_criteria` call it when building `include_<key>` for project ids; specify that the YAML gate is regenerated via the CI drift/parity gate (already planned under 'Routing derivation/validation pipeline') which calls the same function. This makes the sanitization authority a single function rather than a prose rule.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split the editor live-preview (AC 9 / Build Order step 4) into a separate child ticket with its own spike → design → implement sequence, so a spike failure or async-fallback rework does not block delivery of the core criterion-registry and eval-runner capabilities.
findings.finding: The editor live-preview feature (AC 9 / Build Order step 4) is a structurally independent deliverable — it introduces a net-new HTTP endpoint (`POST /criterion/preview`), a spike gate on sync-execution feasibility with an async-fallback design, a dependency on the `agents` extra and credentials, and its own bounded-timeout contract — yet it shares the same AC list and no child ticket boundary separates it from the core registry work. This creates a 'one epic' delivery risk where a UI/preview spike failure can stall the core criterion-registry capability.
findings.location: Build order step 4 + AC 9 (editor live-preview) + 'What exists vs. what we build' § Editor live must-fire/must-not-fire preview

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the open-ended spike with a pre-decided architecture: either commit to the sync shape (with the named reference model and the 60 s timeout as the acceptance bar) or commit to async job+poll from the start, and remove 'A spike first validates sync-execution feasibility' from the AC.
findings.finding: The editor live-preview AC defers the sync-vs-async architectural decision to the executor via an explicit spike ('A spike first validates sync-execution feasibility'), and leaves 'the reference model' unspecified — an agent cannot know which model to benchmark against or what to build without completing the spike first.
findings.location: AC: 'The visual editor authors a project criterion … A spike first validates sync-execution feasibility.' / § What exists vs. what we build (editor preview bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: 'The endpoint is synchronous with a 60 s timeout against <named model, e.g. claude-3-5-sonnet>; if a prototype shows p95 latency > 45 s on that model before this AC is written, switch to async and update this AC.' Remove the open spike gate from the AC itself.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split the `effective_criteria` AC (or add a sub-bullet) to distinguish plan-review callsites (in scope now) from code-review callsites (gated on `b744`), matching the build order and dependency declaration.
findings.finding: The `effective_criteria` AC says it must be 'routed through every vocabulary callsite (see Migration)' without distinguishing plan-review-now callsites from code-review callsites that the Dependency section explicitly gates on `b744`. Migration lists code-review callsites (`OVERLAY_IDS`, `routing_index`, `threshold_for`) alongside plan-review ones, so an implementer following the AC literally would attempt code-review routing before b744 lands — directly contradicting the dependency declaration.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical to the AC: 'routed through every plan-review vocabulary callsite now (load_criteria, check_registry_coverage, attest.registry_version, workflow_ops vocab); code-review callsites (OVERLAY_IDS, routing_index, threshold_for) routed post-b744 per Build order step 5.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the editor preview AC to cover both the sync-feasible case (current shape) AND the async-fallback case (job_id + poll endpoint shape), so the AC remains verifiable regardless of spike outcome.
findings.finding: The editor spike gate says 'if a single sync preview does not return within the timeout… fall back to an async job+poll shape rather than blocking the editor's ThreadingHTTPServer handler', but the corresponding AC fixes a synchronous response shape `{verdict: "fire"|"no-fire", finding?, rationale}` with no async variant. If the spike concludes async is required, the AC shape is wrong and the deliverable definition changes — a cross-section contradiction between the editor-preview design and its acceptance criterion.
findings.location: § What exists vs. what we build — editor live-preview paragraph; AC: 'The visual editor authors a project criterion…' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Revise the AC to read: 'If sync feasibility is confirmed: POST /criterion/preview → {verdict, finding?, rationale}. If async fallback is required per the spike gate: POST /criterion/preview → {job_id} + GET /criterion/preview/{job_id} → {status, verdict?, finding?, rationale?}.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the async job+poll fallback shape for `POST /criterion/preview`: specify the endpoint URL(s), job-id format, poll response schema (`{job_id, status: 'pending'|'done'|'error', result?: {verdict, finding?, rationale}}` or equivalent), and state that the editor frontend must handle both sync and async paths.
findings.finding: The plan references a sync-execution spike with 'fall back to an async job+poll shape rather than blocking the editor's ThreadingHTTPServer handler' — but the async fallback shape is entirely unspecified. No endpoint names, no job-id schema, no poll response schema are defined.
findings.location: § What exists vs. what we build — 'Editor live must-fire/must-not-fire preview' bullet; AC: 'POST /criterion/preview … Spike gate: if a single sync preview does not return within the timeout … fall back to an async job+poll shape'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'spike fallback shape' subsection: `POST /criterion/preview` returns `{job_id: str}` immediately when async; `GET /criterion/preview/<job_id>` returns `{status: 'pending'|'done'|'error', result?: {verdict: 'fire'|'no-fire', finding?: <finding>, rationale: str}}`. Alternatively, if the spike validates sync is feasible within the 60s timeout, explicitly state the async path is dropped and the plan resolves to sync-only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Map each AC to its build-order step (e.g., tag each AC with `[Step N]`) so phase boundaries are unambiguous and reviewers can verify each milestone independently.
findings.finding: The 12 ACs are not mapped to the 5 build-order steps, so it is impossible to determine what 'done' means for each phase. An implementer cannot tell which ACs constitute the MVP (step 1) versus later steps (3–5), creating delivery risk: the epic could be declared done at step 1 while leaving ACs 7, 9, 10, 11 unaddressed — or conversely, step 1 could be blocked waiting on step 4's scope.
findings.location: Build order (risk-ordered MVP; clears G5 thin-slice) + Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `[Step N]` tag to each AC line matching the Build Order numbering, e.g. AC 1 (`effective_criteria`) → `[Step 1]`, AC 5 (DET invariant) → `[Step 2]`, ACs 8/10 (eval runner/routing pipeline) → `[Step 3]`, AC 9 (editor preview) → `[Step 4]`, AC 7 (shared layer) → `[Step 5 / post-b744]`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split this AC into two: (1) an in-scope AC for the plan-review-first `rebar.llm.criteria` layer (completable in this session), and (2) a separate AC or follow-on ticket for the post-b744 code-review adoption, so the DoD for this plan is fully in-session-evaluable.
findings.finding: The shared `rebar.llm.criteria` unification AC is explicitly split across this epic and a future post-b744 session ('this AC is the post-b744 unification child'), making the code-review adoption half not completable or verifiable in the session that closes this plan.
findings.location: AC: 'A shared `rebar.llm.criteria` layer … Gated on `b744`: built against plan-review first and adopted by code-review once b744 lands (this AC is the post-b744 unification child).'
findings.suggested_fix: AC (this plan): 'A `rebar.llm.criteria` module is introduced and consumed by plan-review, with the public interface and routing contract documented so code-review can adopt it after b744 lands.' Move the code-review adoption to a child of b744 or a follow-on ticket.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the routing derivation/validation pipeline (load-time validation + CI drift/parity gate) as an explicit step in the build order with a sequencing rationale (e.g., after step 1 MVP or step 2 DET generalization).
findings.finding: The routing derivation/validation pipeline (a CI drift/parity gate) is listed as net-new work in '§ What exists vs. what we build' and has its own AC, but no corresponding step appears in the 5-step build order. Every other major deliverable maps to a build order step; this one is orphaned from the sequencing plan.
findings.location: § Build order; § What exists vs. what we build (last bullet); AC: 'A routing derivation/validation pipeline…' bullet

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the CI drift/parity gate (AC #11) to the docs deliverable: document what it checks, when it fires, and the remediation command (e.g., `rebar criteria routing generate` or equivalent), within the CLAUDE.md how-to section.
findings.finding: The new CI drift/parity gate for routing derivation/validation (AC #11) is not included in the documentation deliverable, leaving project maintainers without guidance on what failures mean or how to resolve them.
findings.location: AC #11 (routing derivation/validation pipeline) vs. Docs deliverable / Migration & compatibility section
findings.suggested_fix: In the Docs deliverable bullet, append: '… and a description of the routing drift/parity CI gate: what triggers it, what the regenerate command is, and how to interpret diff output.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
